ollama_format.description: Structured smoke output schema for one text response.
ollama_format.title: StructuredSmokeText

structured_output_schema.description: Structured smoke output schema for one text response.
structured_output_schema.title: StructuredSmokeText

response.clean_text:
{  "response_text": "The average number of destocked livestock begins at zero during the initial phase of the simulation, reflecting stable pasture conditions and effective grazing management by all households. At timestep 30, a sudden system-wide destocking event occurs, with all households recording an average of 6.0 destocked livestock. This sharp increase suggests a widespread stressor, likely compounded by stochastic rainfall fluctuations (rain-std=100 and global-rain?=True) reducing biomass availability.   Patrilineal bursts of destocking variability emerge thereafter, with values oscillating between ~4.0 and ~7.0 across subsequent timesteps. Some households resort to drastic reductions (e.g., 4.0) during severe biomass crashes, while others maintain moderate levels (~6.0), possibly reflecting the risk-sensitive herd adjustment strategies of E-RO agents (behavioral-type=E-RO). Over the final half of the simulation (timesteps 70\u2013100), destocking averages stabilize near 4.0\u20134.5, indicating adaptive equilibrium as herds stabilize under combined resource scarcity and environmental stochasticity. Despite the stochasticity in rainfall (rain-mean=200, rainenable), the lack of learning-capacity agents (all E-RO agents) prevents strategy diffusion or adaptive innovation, constraining long-term resilience. The absence of pasture rest mechanisms (resting?=False) exacerbates degradation over time, with herd reductions stabilizing as grazing systems reach new resource-capacity thresholds."
  }

parsed_response.response_text: The average number of destocked livestock begins at zero during the initial phase of the simulation, reflecting stable pasture conditions and effective grazing management by all households. At timestep 30, a sudden system-wide destocking event occurs, with all households recording an average of 6.0 destocked livestock. This sharp increase suggests a widespread stressor, likely compounded by stochastic rainfall fluctuations (rain-std=100 and global-rain?=True) reducing biomass availability.   Patrilineal bursts of destocking variability emerge thereafter, with values oscillating between ~4.0 and ~7.0 across subsequent timesteps. Some households resort to drastic reductions (e.g., 4.0) during severe biomass crashes, while others maintain moderate levels (~6.0), possibly reflecting the risk-sensitive herd adjustment strategies of E-RO agents (behavioral-type=E-RO). Over the final half of the simulation (timesteps 70–100), destocking averages stabilize near 4.0–4.5, indicating adaptive equilibrium as herds stabilize under combined resource scarcity and environmental stochasticity. Despite the stochasticity in rainfall (rain-mean=200, rainenable), the lack of learning-capacity agents (all E-RO agents) prevents strategy diffusion or adaptive innovation, constraining long-term resilience. The absence of pasture rest mechanisms (resting?=False) exacerbates degradation over time, with herd reductions stabilizing as grazing systems reach new resource-capacity thresholds.

message.content:
{  "response_text": "The average number of destocked livestock begins at zero during the initial phase of the simulation, reflecting stable pasture conditions and effective grazing management by all households. At timestep 30, a sudden system-wide destocking event occurs, with all households recording an average of 6.0 destocked livestock. This sharp increase suggests a widespread stressor, likely compounded by stochastic rainfall fluctuations (rain-std=100 and global-rain?=True) reducing biomass availability.   Patrilineal bursts of destocking variability emerge thereafter, with values oscillating between ~4.0 and ~7.0 across subsequent timesteps. Some households resort to drastic reductions (e.g., 4.0) during severe biomass crashes, while others maintain moderate levels (~6.0), possibly reflecting the risk-sensitive herd adjustment strategies of E-RO agents (behavioral-type=E-RO). Over the final half of the simulation (timesteps 70\u2013100), destocking averages stabilize near 4.0\u20134.5, indicating adaptive equilibrium as herds stabilize under combined resource scarcity and environmental stochasticity. Despite the stochasticity in rainfall (rain-mean=200, rainenable), the lack of learning-capacity agents (all E-RO agents) prevents strategy diffusion or adaptive innovation, constraining long-term resilience. The absence of pasture rest mechanisms (resting?=False) exacerbates degradation over time, with herd reductions stabilizing as grazing systems reach new resource-capacity thresholds."
  }

response.raw_text:
{  "response_text": "The average number of destocked livestock begins at zero during the initial phase of the simulation, reflecting stable pasture conditions and effective grazing management by all households. At timestep 30, a sudden system-wide destocking event occurs, with all households recording an average of 6.0 destocked livestock. This sharp increase suggests a widespread stressor, likely compounded by stochastic rainfall fluctuations (rain-std=100 and global-rain?=True) reducing biomass availability.   Patrilineal bursts of destocking variability emerge thereafter, with values oscillating between ~4.0 and ~7.0 across subsequent timesteps. Some households resort to drastic reductions (e.g., 4.0) during severe biomass crashes, while others maintain moderate levels (~6.0), possibly reflecting the risk-sensitive herd adjustment strategies of E-RO agents (behavioral-type=E-RO). Over the final half of the simulation (timesteps 70\u2013100), destocking averages stabilize near 4.0\u20134.5, indicating adaptive equilibrium as herds stabilize under combined resource scarcity and environmental stochasticity. Despite the stochasticity in rainfall (rain-mean=200, rainenable), the lack of learning-capacity agents (all E-RO agents) prevents strategy diffusion or adaptive innovation, constraining long-term resilience. The absence of pasture rest mechanisms (resting?=False) exacerbates degradation over time, with herd reductions stabilizing as grazing systems reach new resource-capacity thresholds."
